patient_demographics.relevant_history: Smoking history contributes to neuroendocrine carcinoma risk in the larynx

treatments.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab (IMpower133 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 IMpower133 trial established carboplatin-etoposide-atezolizumab as standard first-line for ES-SCLC (HR 0.70 for OS, p=0.007). Extrapolation to LCNEC with RB1 loss (SCLC-like molecular subtype) is biologically rational. No prospective data specifically in laryngeal LCNEC. PD-L1 CPS 10 provides additional rationale for checkpoint blockade.

survival_benefit.rationale: IMpower133: median OS 12.3 months vs 10.3 months (HR 0.70). 5-year OS rate 12% vs 2.6%. Benefit seen independent of PD-L1 status. For RB1-loss LCNEC, SCLC-like biology predicts similar sensitivity. PD-L1 CPS 10 may confer additional benefit from atezolizumab component.

accessibility.rationale: Atezolizumab is FDA-approved for ES-SCLC but not specifically for extrapulmonary LCNEC. Off-label use may be supported by tumor board discussion and institutional policies. Carboplatin-etoposide backbone is universally accessible.

safety_profile.rationale: Safety profile consistent with individual agents. Immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis) in 10-15%. Grade 3-4 neutropenia common. Manageable with appropriate monitoring and steroid protocols.

biomarker_match.rationale: RB1 loss defines SCLC-like molecular subtype of LCNEC, directly supporting use of the SCLC standard-of-care regimen. PD-L1 CPS 10 provides further rationale for checkpoint inhibitor inclusion. IMpower133 showed benefit regardless of PD-L1 status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carboplatin-etoposide provides cytotoxic backbone targeting rapidly dividing neuroendocrine cells. Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint, reactivating T-cell-mediated antitumor immunity. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death may enhance immune checkpoint blockade efficacy. PD-L1 CPS 10 suggests the tumor microenvironment may be susceptible to immune reactivation.

key_evidence.study_name: IMpower133 - First-Line Atezolizumab + Carboplatin/Etoposide in ES-SCLC

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression supportive but not required (benefit seen regardless)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for ES-SCLC; off-label for extrapulmonary LCNEC

treatments.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Concurrent Radiation Therapy (Definitive CRT)

evidence_level.rationale: No Phase 3 RCTs specific to laryngeal LCNEC due to extreme rarity. Treatment extrapolated from SCLC paradigm (Phase 3 data) and NCCN guidelines for extrapulmonary poorly differentiated NEC recommending concurrent/sequential chemoradiation for locoregional disease. Retrospective series of laryngeal/pharyngeal high-grade NEC show 78% complete response rate to concurrent platinum-based CRT.

survival_benefit.rationale: Retrospective data for laryngeal high-grade NEC treated with CRT: median DFS 13.1 months, though 80% develop recurrence. For extrapulmonary poorly differentiated NEC, cisplatin-etoposide achieves ORR 41.5% with median OS 15 months. Organ preservation achieved in >67% of T3 laryngeal cancers treated with CRT. 3-year survival for laryngeal LCNEC only ~11%.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin, etoposide, and radiation therapy are universally available at all cancer centers worldwide. FDA-approved and guideline-recommended standard regimen.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 hematological toxicity in 60% of patients. Grade 3-4 GI toxicity in 40%. Radiation-induced mucositis, dysphagia, and laryngeal edema are significant. Preserves larynx function compared to total laryngectomy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Ki-67 70% and RB1 loss indicate SCLC-like biology, which is the primary basis for using platinum-etoposide. Synaptophysin+/chromogranin+ confirm neuroendocrine histology warranting SCLC-paradigm treatment.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms DNA crosslinks causing apoptosis in rapidly dividing cells. Etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II, preventing DNA unwinding and replication. Concurrent radiation provides local tumor control through direct DNA damage. The combination exploits the high proliferative rate (Ki-67 70%) of this tumor.

key_evidence.study_name: Retrospective analysis of laryngeal/hypopharyngeal high-grade NEC (single-center)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (standard chemotherapy agents); NCCN-recommended approach

treatments.name: Total Laryngectomy + Neck Dissection + Adjuvant Cisplatin/Etoposide

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective laryngeal LCNEC series show 90% present at Stage IV with 80% nodal metastases. Surgery with adjuvant chemotherapy is established for resectable pulmonary LCNEC (JCOG1205). For T3N1 disease, total laryngectomy with bilateral neck dissection followed by platinum-etoposide is an evidence-supported approach. NCCN recommends surgical resection when feasible for extrapulmonary high-grade NEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Surgical resection is the strongest independent prognostic factor for laryngeal NEC survival. For localized LCNEC, surgery + adjuvant chemo achieves 2-year recurrence-free survival of 65-69% (JCOG1205/1206). Upfront surgery may offer superior local control compared to CRT for high-grade NEC, though the trade-off is loss of the larynx.

accessibility.rationale: Total laryngectomy is widely available at head and neck surgery centers. Requires experienced head and neck surgical team. Adjuvant platinum-etoposide is universally available.

safety_profile.rationale: Permanent loss of natural voice and normal swallowing. Requires permanent tracheostomy. Significant impact on quality of life including social isolation, speech difficulties, and dyspnea. Surgical complications include pharyngocutaneous fistula (15-25%), wound infection, and stomal stenosis.

biomarker_match.rationale: RB1 loss and Ki-67 70% indicate extremely aggressive tumor biology where definitive surgical resection may be warranted to achieve complete local control before systemic dissemination. T3N1 stage is borderline for organ preservation vs. surgery.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Total laryngectomy achieves complete surgical extirpation of the primary tumor and involved lymph nodes. Adjuvant cisplatin-etoposide targets micrometastatic disease and reduces systemic recurrence risk, addressing the high metastatic potential of LCNEC (75% mortality at 2 years without systemic therapy).

key_evidence.study_name: JCOG1205/1206 - Adjuvant Chemotherapy for Resected High-Grade Pulmonary NEC

notable_side_effects: Permanent loss of voice (requires tracheoesophageal prosthesis or electrolarynx)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (standard surgical and chemotherapy approach)

treatments.name: Irinotecan/Cisplatin (IP Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 JCOG1213 compared cisplatin-irinotecan vs cisplatin-etoposide in advanced NEC of the digestive system. Phase 3 JCOG1205/1206 for resected high-grade pulmonary NEC showed no significant difference in 2-year RFS between EP (65.4%) and IP (69%). NCCN lists both EP and IP as category 1 options for high-grade NEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: First-line irinotecan-cisplatin in LCNEC: ORR 54.5%, median PFS 5.9 months, median OS 15.1 months. Comparable to cisplatin-etoposide with potentially different toxicity profile. May offer alternative for patients intolerant to etoposide.

accessibility.rationale: Both irinotecan and cisplatin are generic, widely available, and inexpensive. FDA-approved for multiple indications. Available at all cancer treatment facilities.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant diarrhea risk (irinotecan-specific). Grade 3-4 diarrhea in 15-20%. Less myelosuppression than EP but more GI toxicity. Cisplatin nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity remain. UGT1A1 polymorphism testing recommended.

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all confirmed high-grade NEC regardless of specific molecular markers. Ki-67 70% supports use of aggressive platinum-based chemotherapy. UGT1A1 genotyping recommended for irinotecan dosing.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Irinotecan is a topoisomerase I inhibitor that prevents DNA re-ligation during replication, causing lethal DNA strand breaks. Combined with cisplatin's DNA crosslinking, the dual targeting of topoisomerase I and DNA repair provides complementary cytotoxic mechanisms against rapidly dividing neuroendocrine cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Multicenter Phase II Study - Irinotecan/Cisplatin for LCNEC

treatments.name: Tarlatamab (DLL3-targeted BiTE — DeLLphi-301/304)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 DeLLphi-301: ORR 40%, median OS 14.3 months in pretreated SCLC. Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 showed statistically significant OS benefit over standard chemotherapy. FDA accelerated approval May 2024 for SCLC. Phase 2 basket trial (UCCC-01/UCLA L-10, NCT06788938) actively enrolling DLL3+ extrapulmonary NEC including head and neck origin. TAURUS trial (NCT06816394) enrolling extrapulmonary NEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: DeLLphi-301: median OS 14.3 months, median PFS 4.9 months, ORR 40% at 10mg dose, DCR 70%. DeLLphi-304: statistically significant OS improvement over chemotherapy in relapsed SCLC. DLL3 expressed in ~74% of LCNEC, providing high likelihood of target availability. Durable responses with median DOR 12.3 months.

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval for ES-SCLC (May 2024). Not approved for extrapulmonary NEC. Available through basket trials (NCT06788938, NCT06816394) at select academic centers. Off-label access may be challenging.

safety_profile.rationale: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) in 52% (mostly grade 1). Requires step-up dosing and hospitalization for first doses. Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) reported. Manageable with prophylactic measures.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expression in 74% of LCNEC and up to 85-96% of SCLC. RB1 loss indicates SCLC-like biology, correlating with higher DLL3 expression. Requires IHC confirmation of DLL3 expression (testing should be performed). Extrapulmonary high-grade NEC show DLL3 expression in 68-81% depending on site.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tarlatamab is a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) antibody that simultaneously binds DLL3 (delta-like ligand 3) on neuroendocrine tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, forming cytolytic synapses that activate T cells to release perforin and granzyme B, causing tumor cell lysis. DLL3 is a Notch pathway ligand highly expressed on neuroendocrine cancer cells but virtually absent from normal adult tissue, providing tumor selectivity.
biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC (must be confirmed)
treatments.availability: FDA Accelerated Approval for ES-SCLC; Clinical Trial for extrapulmonary NEC (NCT06788938, NCT06816394)

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab (Maintenance)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 IMforte trial: lurbinectedin + atezolizumab maintenance after platinum-etoposide + atezolizumab induction significantly improved PFS (5.4 vs 2.1 months) and OS (13.2 vs 10.6 months) in ES-SCLC. FDA approved October 2025 for ES-SCLC maintenance. DeLLphi-303 evaluating tarlatamab + PD-L1 inhibitor maintenance.

survival_benefit.rationale: IMforte: median PFS 5.4 vs 2.1 months, median OS 13.2 vs 10.6 months with lurbinectedin + atezolizumab maintenance. Clinically meaningful improvement in both endpoints. For RB1-loss tumors, lurbinectedin may have enhanced activity.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved October 2025 specifically for ES-SCLC maintenance. Off-label use for extrapulmonary LCNEC not yet established. Requires prior induction with carboplatin-etoposide-atezolizumab. Cost may be substantial.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicities of lurbinectedin (myelosuppression) and atezolizumab (immune-related AEs). Manageable but requires monitoring for both hematologic and immune toxicities. No unexpected safety signals in IMforte.

biomarker_match.rationale: RB1 loss predicts lurbinectedin sensitivity. PD-L1 CPS 10 supports atezolizumab benefit. SCLC-like molecular profile aligns perfectly with the indication studied in IMforte.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Sequential approach: after induction with carboplatin-etoposide-atezolizumab, maintenance with lurbinectedin (selective transcription inhibitor causing RNA polymerase II degradation) + atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1). Lurbinectedin suppresses tumor transcriptional programs while atezolizumab maintains immune surveillance. Synergy exploits both transcriptional addiction and immune evasion of neuroendocrine tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: IMforte Phase 3 Trial - Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab Maintenance in ES-SCLC

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for ES-SCLC maintenance (October 2025); off-label for extrapulmonary NEC

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin (Zepzelca)

evidence_level.rationale: FDA accelerated approval for relapsed ES-SCLC (2020) based on phase 2 basket trial: ORR 35.2%, median DOR 5.3 months, median OS 9.3 months. Phase 3 IMforte: lurbinectedin + atezolizumab maintenance improved PFS (5.4 vs 2.1 months) and OS (13.2 vs 10.6 months). RB1 loss has been identified as a potential predictor of lurbinectedin benefit.

survival_benefit.rationale: Second-line SCLC: median OS 9.3 months, median PFS 3.5 months. Sensitive relapse (CTFI >=90 days): ORR 44%, DOR >=6 months in 44%. For RB1-loss tumors, emerging data suggest enhanced sensitivity. Applicable for this patient at relapse after first-line platinum-etoposide.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for relapsed ES-SCLC. Off-label use for extrapulmonary LCNEC requires tumor board support. Available at most US cancer centers. Lurbinectedin + atezolizumab maintenance FDA approved October 2025 for ES-SCLC.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable toxicity profile. Main adverse effects are hematological (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia). Grade 3-4 neutropenia in ~40%. Less emetogenic than cisplatin-based regimens. Single-agent convenience of administration.

biomarker_match.rationale: RB1 loss and Notch pathway mutations identified as potentially predictive of lurbinectedin benefit. SCLC-like molecular profile (RB1 loss, high Ki-67) directly supports mechanism of action as a selective inhibitor of oncogenic transcription.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lurbinectedin is a selective inhibitor of oncogenic transcription that triggers degradation of phosphorylated RNA polymerase II and induces DNA double-strand breaks. It exploits the transcriptional addiction of neuroendocrine tumors, which rely heavily on active transcription for proliferation. RB1-loss tumors may be particularly vulnerable due to dysregulated transcriptional programs.
biomarker_requirements: RB1 loss may predict enhanced sensitivity
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for relapsed ES-SCLC; off-label for extrapulmonary NEC

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (Anti-PD-1)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports and small retrospective series demonstrate checkpoint inhibitor activity in LCNEC with high TMB. KEYNOTE-048 showed pembrolizumab efficacy in PD-L1 CPS>=1 head/neck cancers (median OS 12.3 months). No prospective LCNEC-specific trials. FDA tumor-agnostic approval for TMB-high (>=10 mut/Mb) solid tumors may apply if TMB is confirmed high.

survival_benefit.rationale: In LCNEC case series, 60% partial response rate among PD-L1+ patients treated with nivolumab/pembrolizumab. Median PFS 57 weeks in responsive LCNEC patients. Single case report showed complete response even in PD-L1-negative LCNEC with high TMB. PD-L1 CPS 10 in this patient is favorable.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab FDA-approved for multiple indications including PD-L1 CPS>=1 head/neck SCC (KEYNOTE-048) and TMB-high solid tumors. Off-label use for NEC may be covered depending on PD-L1 CPS and TMB status.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Immune-related adverse events mostly grade 1-2. Less myelosuppression, nausea, and alopecia than chemotherapy. Manageable with steroid protocols.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS 10 supports potential benefit from anti-PD-1 therapy. RB1-loss tumors may have higher TMB. Neuroendocrine carcinomas in smokers often have elevated TMB due to smoking-related mutational burden.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T-cell-mediated antitumor immunity. PD-L1 CPS 10 indicates moderate immune cell infiltration and PD-L1 expression in the tumor microenvironment, suggesting susceptibility to checkpoint blockade.

key_evidence.journal: Journal for ImmunoTherapy of Cancer / Journal of Clinical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: TMB-high status may further support use
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for PD-L1+ head/neck SCC and TMB-high solid tumors; off-label for NEC

treatments.name: Topotecan (Second-Line)

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved for relapsed SCLC with established Phase 3 data. For extrapulmonary NEC, retrospective data shows disease control rate of 23% with topotecan. RESILIENT Phase 3 compared liposomal irinotecan vs topotecan in relapsed SCLC (median OS 8.3 months topotecan arm).

survival_benefit.rationale: In extrapulmonary NEC: median PFS 2.1 months, median OS 4.1 months with topotecan. In SCLC: median OS 8.3 months second-line. Modest activity but represents established second-line option. Response rates are limited in extrapulmonary NEC (23% DCR).

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for relapsed SCLC. Generic formulation widely available and inexpensive. Available at all oncology treatment centers. Oral and IV formulations available.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 neutropenia is dose-limiting (70-80%). Grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia in 25-30%. Anemia requiring transfusion common. Manageable with dose adjustments and growth factor support.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-specific; applies to all high-grade NEC as second-line therapy. SCLC-like biology (RB1 loss) supports SCLC treatment paradigm extrapolation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Topotecan inhibits topoisomerase I, preventing DNA religation during replication and causing lethal DNA single-strand breaks. Highly active in rapidly proliferating tumors. Particularly relevant for Ki-67 70% tumors where high replication rate increases dependence on topoisomerase I activity.

key_evidence.study_name: Topotecan in pretreated metastatic extrapulmonary NEC

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for relapsed SCLC (generic available)

treatments.name: Temozolomide + Capecitabine (CAPTEM)

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective data supports CAPTEM in G3 well-differentiated NET (ORR 26.5%, DCR 76.2%, median PFS 12 months). For poorly differentiated NEC with Ki-67 >55%, temozolomide monotherapy shows limited efficacy (ORR 18%, median PFS 5.86 months, median OS 12.1 months). NCCN lists CAPTEM as an option for extrapulmonary NEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: CAPTEM does not overcome the aggressive biology of poorly differentiated NEC with Ki-67 >55%. Temozolomide monotherapy in extrapulmonary NEC: ORR 18%, median PFS 5.86 months, median OS 12.1 months. Less effective than platinum-etoposide for high-grade NEC. Best reserved if platinum-refractory.

accessibility.rationale: Both temozolomide and capecitabine are oral medications, offering convenience. Generic formulations available. Widely accessible at all oncology practices. Oral administration allows outpatient treatment.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally better tolerated than platinum-based regimens. Main toxicities: myelosuppression, nausea, fatigue. Oral administration reduces infusion-related complications. Hand-foot syndrome from capecitabine manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Ki-67 70% exceeds the optimal range for CAPTEM response (best outcomes with Ki-67 20-54%). Poorly differentiated NEC histology with Ki-67 >55% shows diminished CAPTEM efficacy. MGMT promoter methylation may predict temozolomide benefit but is not routinely tested in NEC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Temozolomide is an alkylating agent that methylates DNA at guanine residues, triggering mismatch repair-mediated cell death. Capecitabine is a fluoropyrimidine prodrug converted to 5-FU intracellularly, inhibiting thymidylate synthase and DNA synthesis. The combination exploits depletion of MGMT by temozolomide, sensitizing cells to fluoropyrimidine cytotoxicity.

key_evidence.study_name: Temozolomide Phase II in Extrapulmonary NEC (TENEC)

biomarker_requirements: MGMT promoter methylation status may predict benefit
Best for NEC with Ki-67 20-54% (less effective at Ki-67 70%)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (both drugs generic); NCCN-listed option

treatments.name: FOLFOX (5-FU/Leucovorin/Oxaliplatin)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN lists FOLFOX as an alternative regimen for extrapulmonary poorly differentiated NEC. Phase 2/3 data from GI-NEC studies. TOPIC-NEC Phase 3 trial compared EP vs IP in GI-NEC, with FOLFOX used in subsequent lines. Limited data specifically in head and neck NEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: In extrapulmonary NEC, FOLFOX used primarily as second-line or for type I LCNEC (NSCLC-like molecular subtype). This patient's RB1 loss suggests SCLC-like biology, making FOLFOX less optimal than platinum-etoposide. May be considered for platinum-refractory disease.

accessibility.rationale: All components (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin) are generic, widely available, and well-established. Used universally in GI oncology. Available at all cancer centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile from extensive GI cancer use. Oxaliplatin-related peripheral neuropathy is cumulative and dose-limiting. Myelosuppression less severe than platinum-etoposide. Mucositis and diarrhea from 5-FU.

biomarker_match.rationale: Better suited for type I LCNEC (STK11/KEAP1 alterations, NSCLC-like). RB1 loss in this patient indicates type II (SCLC-like) LCNEC, which responds better to SCLC regimens. FOLFOX is a reasonable backup option but not first-choice for this molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Oxaliplatin forms platinum-DNA adducts causing replication arrest and apoptosis. 5-Fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase, blocking DNA synthesis. Leucovorin enhances 5-FU binding to thymidylate synthase. This regimen offers a different cytotoxic mechanism for tumors resistant to etoposide-based regimens.

key_evidence.study_name: NCCN Guidelines - Extrapulmonary NEC / Retrospective Series

biomarker_requirements: May be preferred for type I (NSCLC-like) LCNEC molecular subtype

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Blockade)

evidence_level.rationale: DART trial (SWOG S1609, NCT02834013) includes cohort for extrapulmonary NEC with nivolumab + ipilimumab. CheckMate trials established dual checkpoint blockade in multiple tumor types. SVV-001 Phase I/II trial combining oncolytic virus with nivolumab + ipilimumab in high-grade NEC is recruiting. Limited prospective data specifically in LCNEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: In LCNEC case series, nivolumab achieved median PFS of 57 weeks (13+ months) in responders with 60% ORR among PD-L1+ patients. Addition of ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) may enhance response. PD-L1 CPS 10 supports potential benefit. Dual blockade may overcome resistance seen with anti-PD-1 monotherapy.

accessibility.rationale: Nivolumab + ipilimumab approved for multiple tumor types but not NEC-specific. DART trial provides access at SWOG sites. Off-label combination challenging to access for NEC. Requires prior authorization and tumor board support.

safety_profile.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade significantly increases immune-related adverse events (40-60% grade 3-4 irAEs). Immune-related colitis (15-20%), hepatitis (10-15%), pneumonitis, and endocrinopathies. Requires experienced immunotherapy management team.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS 10 supports anti-PD-1 component. RB1-loss LCNEC may have higher TMB, enhancing immunotherapy benefit. Smoking history associated with higher mutational burden. CTLA-4 blockade adds complementary mechanism targeting regulatory T cells.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab blocks PD-1 on T cells, preventing tumor-mediated immune suppression. Ipilimumab blocks CTLA-4 on T cells, enhancing T-cell priming and activation in lymph nodes. The combination targets both the effector and priming phases of the anti-tumor immune response, potentially overcoming immune evasion mechanisms in neuroendocrine carcinomas.

key_evidence.study_name: DART Trial (SWOG S1609) - NEC Cohort + LCNEC Immunotherapy Retrospective Series

biomarker_requirements: RB1 loss may predict enhanced benefit

treatments.name: Obrixtamig (BI 764532, DLL3/CD3 T-cell Engager)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 data in advanced pulmonary and extrapulmonary neuroendocrine carcinomas (Boehringer Ingelheim). Early results show clinical activity in DLL3-expressing tumors. Broader DLL3/CD3 platform than tarlatamab with potential for expanded NEC indications. Still in early-phase development.

survival_benefit.rationale: Early phase 1 data shows clinical responses in NEC patients. Mechanism of action similar to tarlatamab (DLL3/CD3 BiTE). Expected to provide comparable efficacy in DLL3+ tumors. Full survival data not yet mature.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through Phase 1 clinical trials at select academic centers. Not yet FDA approved. Boehringer Ingelheim conducting trials for pulmonary and extrapulmonary NEC. Limited enrollment sites.

safety_profile.rationale: Similar CRS risk profile to tarlatamab class. Step-up dosing required. Expected manageable safety profile based on DLL3-BiTE class experience. Phase 1 safety data emerging.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets DLL3, expressed in 74% of LCNEC. RB1 loss correlates with higher DLL3 expression. Specifically designed for neuroendocrine carcinomas with DLL3 expression. Broader tumor type eligibility than tarlatamab.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Obrixtamig is a next-generation DLL3/CD3 bispecific T-cell engager developed by Boehringer Ingelheim. It binds DLL3 on neuroendocrine tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, inducing T-cell-mediated cytolysis. Designed to potentially broaden the reach of DLL3-targeted therapy to extrapulmonary neuroendocrine carcinomas including head and neck primaries.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 1 Study of Obrixtamig in Advanced NEC

treatments.name: Rovalpituzumab Tesirine (Rova-T, DLL3-targeted ADC)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I/II study in DLL3-expressing solid tumors including NEC/NET (101 patients with NEC). ORR 13% in NEC/NET at recommended dose (0.3 mg/kg). Phase 3 TAHOE study in SCLC failed to meet primary OS endpoint vs topotecan. Development program largely discontinued for SCLC but DLL3-ADC concept remains relevant.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 13% in NEC/NET cohort with confirmed responses in 15/145 patients. Phase 3 TAHOE showed inferior OS compared to topotecan. Limited survival benefit demonstrated. DLL3-ADC concept validated but this specific molecule has limitations.

accessibility.rationale: Development largely discontinued after Phase 3 failure in SCLC. Not commercially available. May be accessible through compassionate use or investigator-initiated studies. Superseded by tarlatamab as DLL3-targeting approach.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3/4 adverse events: anemia 17%, thrombocytopenia 15%, elevated AST 8%. Photosensitivity (pyrrolobenzodiazepine payload). Serosal effusions were dose-limiting. Hepatotoxicity was a concern. Worse safety profile than tarlatamab.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expressed in 74% of LCNEC and 65-81% of extrapulmonary high-grade NEC. RB1 loss correlates with DLL3 expression. Mechanism is DLL3-dependent regardless of specific NEC primary site.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Rovalpituzumab tesirine is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) comprising a DLL3-targeting monoclonal antibody linked to a pyrrolobenzodiazepine (PBD) DNA crosslinking payload via a protease-cleavable linker. Upon binding DLL3 on tumor cells, it is internalized and releases PBD dimers that form interstrand DNA crosslinks, causing cell death independent of cell cycle phase.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase I/II Study of Rova-T in DLL3-Expressing Advanced Solid Tumors

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC (>=1% tumor cells)
treatments.availability: Research Only / Compassionate Use (development largely discontinued)

treatments.name: SVV-001 (Seneca Valley Virus) + Nivolumab/Ipilimumab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I/II clinical trial initiated by Seneca Therapeutics for high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas. First randomized trial of SVV-001 oncolytic virus with immunotherapy. Preclinical rationale for oncolytic virus + checkpoint inhibitor synergy in NEC. Very early stage of development.

survival_benefit.rationale: No mature clinical survival data available. Preclinical models show oncolytic virus-mediated tumor lysis combined with enhanced immune activation. Theoretical synergy with checkpoint blockade. Outcome data awaited.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through Phase I/II clinical trial. Very limited enrollment sites. Experimental agent not available outside of trials. Seneca Therapeutics sponsoring.

safety_profile.rationale: SVV-001 has shown acceptable safety in prior SCLC studies. Combined with nivolumab + ipilimumab adds significant immune toxicity risk (40-60% grade 3-4 irAEs). Overall safety profile uncertain for combination.

biomarker_match.rationale: SVV-001 specifically targets neuroendocrine tumors expressing anthrax toxin receptor (ANTXR1). High-grade NEC histology is the target population. RB1 loss and Ki-67 70% consistent with aggressive NEC targeted by this approach.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: SVV-001 (Seneca Valley Virus) is a naturally occurring oncolytic picornavirus that selectively infects and lyses neuroendocrine cancer cells via ANTXR1 (anthrax toxin receptor 1). Viral oncolysis releases tumor antigens and danger signals, creating an immunogenic tumor microenvironment. Combined with nivolumab (anti-PD-1) and ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4), this aims to convert immunologically cold neuroendocrine tumors into hot tumors susceptible to immune attack.

key_evidence.study_name: SVV-001 + Nivolumab/Ipilimumab Phase I/II in High-Grade NEC

biomarker_requirements: High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma or Grade 3 NET
notable_side_effects: Grade 3-4 immune-related adverse events (nivolumab + ipilimumab combination)

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Basket Trial of Tarlatamab in Patients with Advanced DLL3-Expressing Tumors (UCCC-01/UCLA L-10)
clinical_trials.early_results: Enrolling; tarlatamab showed 40% ORR in DeLLphi-301 (SCLC). Extrapulmonary NEC patients have shown responses to tarlatamab in single-center experience.

clinical_trials.title: Tarlatamab for Advanced Extrapulmonary Small Cell Carcinoma and Neuroendocrine Carcinoma (TAURUS)
biomarker_requirements: Extrapulmonary NEC (GI, GU, gynecologic, head and neck, or unknown primary)
clinical_trials.early_results: Newly initiated trial specifically targeting extrapulmonary NEC including head and neck primaries. Based on DeLLphi-301/304 data in SCLC.

clinical_trials.title: Dual Anti-CTLA-4 & Anti-PD-1 Blockade in Rare Tumors (DART / SWOG S1609)
biomarker_requirements: Rare tumor cohorts including extrapulmonary NEC
clinical_trials.early_results: Basket trial with cohort for extrapulmonary NEC. Nivolumab + ipilimumab combination. Interim results for NEC cohort pending.

clinical_trials.title: Enasidenib for Sinonasal Undifferentiated Carcinoma, LCNEC, and Other Head/Neck Tumors
biomarker_requirements: Sinonasal or head and neck NEC histology
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating oral IDH2 inhibitor enasidenib in head and neck neuroendocrine and undifferentiated carcinomas. Early enrollment phase.

clinical_trials.title: SVV-001 Oncolytic Virus + Nivolumab/Ipilimumab in High-Grade Neuroendocrine Carcinomas
clinical_trials.early_results: First randomized trial of oncolytic virus + immunotherapy in NEC. SVV-001 selectively infects neuroendocrine tumor cells. Enrollment ongoing.

clinical_trials.title: Tarlatamab + PD-L1 Inhibitor as First-Line Maintenance After Chemo-Immunotherapy in ES-SCLC (DeLLphi-303)
biomarker_requirements: ES-SCLC (primary indication); extrapulmonary NEC may be considered
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating tarlatamab + atezolizumab maintenance after platinum-etoposide-atezolizumab induction. Safety and preliminary efficacy data being collected.

combination_strategies.rationale: IMpower133 demonstrated OS benefit (HR 0.70) adding atezolizumab to carboplatin-etoposide in ES-SCLC. RB1 loss indicates SCLC-like biology, supporting this combination. PD-L1 CPS 10 provides additional immunotherapy rationale. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint blockade. Standard of care for SCLC, rational extrapolation to SCLC-like LCNEC.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 (IMforte in ES-SCLC, FDA approved Oct 2025)
combination_strategies.rationale: After achieving response with induction chemotherapy + immunotherapy, maintenance with lurbinectedin + atezolizumab improved PFS (5.4 vs 2.1 months) and OS (13.2 vs 10.6 months). Lurbinectedin targets transcriptional addiction of NEC while atezolizumab maintains immune surveillance. RB1 loss may predict enhanced lurbinectedin sensitivity. Sequential strategy maximizes both cytotoxic and maintenance benefits.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 2 (extrapolated from KEYNOTE-689 in HNSCC and PACIFIC in NSCLC)
combination_strategies.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 showed pembrolizumab benefit in perioperative HNSCC setting (PD-L1 CPS >=10). PACIFIC trial paradigm: adjuvant durvalumab after CRT in NSCLC improved OS. For T3N1 laryngeal LCNEC, consolidation immunotherapy after CRT could prevent recurrence. PD-L1 CPS 10 supports checkpoint inhibitor sensitivity. Radiation-induced immunogenic cell death enhances neoantigen presentation for immune attack.

DeLLphi-303 evaluates tarlatamab + PD-L1 inhibitor as maintenance. BiTE-mediated T-cell activation may be sustained and enhanced by concurrent PD-L1 blockade, preventing T-cell exhaustion. DLL3 expression in 74% of LCNEC provides target availability. Combining T-cell engagement with checkpoint release could overcome the immunologically cold microenvironment typical of neuroendocrine tumors.

Dual checkpoint blockade targets both the priming phase (CTLA-4) and effector phase (PD-1) of anti-tumor immunity. DART trial actively enrolling NEC cohort. In LCNEC retrospective data, single-agent checkpoint inhibitors showed 60% ORR in PD-L1+ patients. Dual blockade expected to enhance response rate and durability. Smoker status and PD-L1 CPS 10 favor immunotherapy response.

supportive_care.evidence: ASHA guidelines and systematic reviews of post-laryngeal cancer treatment rehabilitation. Multidisciplinary supportive care studies show improved functional outcomes and quality of life with early speech-language pathology intervention.
supportive_care.benefit: If laryngectomy: tracheoesophageal prosthesis (TEP) enables voice restoration in 80-90% of patients. If CRT: swallowing therapy reduces aspiration risk and dysphagia severity. Early prophylactic exercises before and during treatment improve long-term swallowing function.

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Support with PEG Tube Placement
supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines for cancer nutrition. Prospective studies show prophylactic PEG placement reduces treatment interruptions and malnutrition in head and neck cancer patients receiving CRT. Weight loss >5% during treatment associated with worse survival outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains nutritional status during CRT-induced mucositis and dysphagia. Reduces treatment interruptions by 30-40%. Prevents significant weight loss associated with poor treatment outcomes. Particularly important during concurrent cisplatin-etoposide and radiation.

supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses demonstrate continued smoking during cancer treatment reduces treatment efficacy, increases complications, and worsens survival. For head and neck cancer patients, smoking cessation improves radiation response rates and reduces second primary tumor risk by 50%.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved treatment response (radiation and chemotherapy efficacy). Reduced surgical complications and wound healing issues. Decreased risk of second primary cancers. Improved overall survival (HR 0.63 for cessation vs continued smoking in H&N cancer).

supportive_care.evidence: Depression and anxiety prevalence in head and neck cancer patients is 20-40%. Laryngeal cancer patients face unique psychological burden due to voice loss, facial disfigurement, and social isolation. Randomized trials show cognitive behavioral therapy and supportive counseling improve quality of life and treatment adherence.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved treatment adherence and completion rates. Reduced distress and depression scores. Better coping with voice loss or alteration. Support groups for laryngectomy patients available through organizations like WebWhispers and IAL (International Association of Laryngectomees).

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple meta-analyses and RCTs show structured exercise programs during and after cancer treatment improve fatigue, physical function, and quality of life. Cochrane review of exercise interventions in cancer patients demonstrates consistent benefit across tumor types.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced cancer-related fatigue (effect size 0.3-0.5). Improved cardiopulmonary fitness to tolerate platinum-based chemotherapy. Maintained functional status during treatment. May improve immune function and treatment outcomes.

supportive_care.evidence: PCI is standard in limited-stage SCLC achieving complete response, reducing brain metastasis incidence from 40% to 15% and improving OS. For extrapulmonary high-grade NEC with SCLC-like biology (RB1 loss), brain metastasis risk is elevated but PCI data is extrapolated. NCCN does not specifically recommend PCI for extrapulmonary NEC.
supportive_care.benefit: Potential reduction in brain metastasis risk, which is significant in SCLC-like NEC. Must weigh against neurocognitive toxicity. Consider MRI surveillance as alternative. Decision should be individualized based on response to initial treatment.
